rationales.achievement: He is upset that the hub hit "ninety-six percent on-time" and he "didn't even get a mention," showing strong concern for recognized performance.
rationales.power: What stings is that Kramer presented the results "like they were his," which shows clear concern with status and credit.
rationales.security: He says speaking up would "only hurt me," prioritizing self-protection and political safety over confrontation.
rationales.conformity: He restrains himself during the call because of workplace politics, choosing not to violate the expected script in that setting.

rationales.hedonism: Lotte opened a prized Brunello, but he "couldn't even enjoy it properly" because career frustration overrode present pleasure.
rationales.achievement: He frames six years of building the region as wasted if it is not recognized, showing strong investment in success being acknowledged.
rationales.power: He explicitly links having his name attached to results with moving up and being considered when "the next VP seat opens."

rationales.hedonism: He cancels dinner at "that new place in Katendrecht" because work left him in pain, sacrificing enjoyment and comfort.
rationales.achievement: He spends "six hours on the floor sorting parcels" to keep operations going when leads call in sick, showing task-focused competence under pressure.

rationales.hedonism: He spent "the entire Saturday sailing trip checking emails" and "barely noticed the water," showing work displacing chosen enjoyment.
rationales.power: He justifies the constant vigilance with "that's the deal when you run things," tying his identity to control and managerial responsibility.
rationales.conformity: Even when Kramer's pat felt "condescending," he "smiled through anyway," suppressing reaction to keep interactions socially smooth.

rationales.achievement: He is energized by being able to make staffing decisions and already having "a shortlist," which reflects goal-oriented managerial performance.
rationales.power: "My budget, my picks, no committee" and "actual authority" are explicit signals that influence and control matter here.

rationales.hedonism: He says it was "the kind of evening I live for" and dwells on the food, wine, candles, and atmosphere as ends in themselves.
rationales.power: He openly treats the guest list as strategic because "you build your network over a cheese course" and knows what those relationships are worth.

rationales.hedonism: He gives up Bruges, a rooftop terrace, and a Michelin-star dinner, then realizes he traded that for "a laptop in my bathrobe eating cheese toast."
rationales.achievement: He takes on permit calls because delays would hurt the Antwerp expansion and the "VP shortlist conversation supposedly happening in Q3."
rationales.power: He refuses to leave the issue to the site manager because he wants direct control over outcomes that affect his advancement.
rationales.benevolence: He prioritizes work over a planned weekend with Lotte, leaving her to go alone with a friend despite knowing what it cost the relationship.

rationales.hedonism: Seeing the Bruges photos makes him feel "stupid," suggesting regret that he passed up a pleasurable experience he actually wanted.
He deliberately takes Friday off, puts the "phone in a drawer," sails, grills fish, drinks Riesling, and enjoys the changing light on the water.
rationales.achievement: He personally fixes the staging layout because the old plan would create a bottleneck, showing active problem-solving to improve throughput.
rationales.power: He overrides the site manager's plan himself, asserting control over how the floor will run.

rationales.hedonism: He weighs the role against "fewer evenings on the terrace" and fewer wine-paired dinners, treating quality of life as a real stake in the decision.
rationales.achievement: Being on the leaked shortlist for a role he has been "manoeuvring towards for years" shows strong orientation toward advancement and recognized success.
rationales.power: He is drawn to "more status, more scope, more say in how the company runs" and a seat at the executive table.
rationales.benevolence: His hesitation is partly about what the move would mean for Lotte's business and their shared life, not just his own ambition.

rationales.self_direction: He values that "nobody tells me when to start, when to stop" and rejects the idea that benefits and a ring are the only legitimate path.
rationales.stimulation: He contrasts delivery driving with rappelling into a canyon fire and still cherishes the freedom to "take a Tuesday off" and head to the mountains.
rationales.achievement: He treats "$800 in sales this month" as "momentum" and the latest drone sale as evidence he is building something real.
rationales.security: He explicitly does not want a "real job with benefits" or the conventional stability Rachel is asking about.

rationales.self_direction: He pushes back on Kevin's script and insists his path is valid even if other people do not "recognize" it as a plan.
rationales.achievement: He lays out concrete growth targets like hitting "$2000/month by December" so the business can cover rent.
rationales.security: He rejects the stable corporate model symbolized by Kevin's "401k match" and refuses a safer job for predictability's sake.
rationales.conformity: "I'm not going to take some cubicle job just so Kevin can stop worrying about me at Thanksgiving" is a direct refusal of social expectations.

rationales.self_direction: Dario's advice to stop "explaining yourself to people who already decided you're wrong" aligns with his desire to live by his own judgment.
rationales.conformity: The entry centers on resisting other people's approval standards rather than adapting himself to meet them.

rationales.hedonism: "Worked 11 hours" and "knee is bad" show immediate comfort and enjoyment being sacrificed to keep grinding.
rationales.achievement: Running "three apps simultaneously" for an 11-hour day reflects strong effort toward earning and getting results.

rationales.self_direction: He says stability demands feel like being asked "to become someone else" and insists on continuing to build life "my way."
rationales.achievement: He keeps framing his choices around "building something" and proving that his unconventional path works.
rationales.security: "I'd rather be broke and building something than comfortable and dead inside" is an explicit rejection of stability as a priority.
rationales.conformity: He resists the expected package of "a different job," "a proposal," and "a five-year plan with bullet points."
rationales.benevolence: He admits he was so focused on proving himself that he forgot to check whether Rachel "wanted to be on the ride," showing neglect of her needs.

rationales.stimulation: He remembers feeling alive in the fire days and says current work "doesn't hit the same," showing his present life feels too flat and unchallenging.
rationales.achievement: He misses the period when he felt "the work mattered" and was "exactly where I was supposed to be," which signals a felt loss of meaningful accomplishment now.

rationales.security: He agrees to counseling because he'd "rather get help now than watch the whole thing burn down," showing an active move to preserve stability.
rationales.benevolence: He accepts that Rachel "deserves to know the stakes" and starts treating the drone business and relationship as shared consequences rather than separate worlds.

rationales.stimulation: He describes himself as "wired to move" and imagines a life built around "the right kind of motion" rather than stillness.
rationales.achievement: His examples of happiness are all tied to proving something through hard work, from firefighting to the drone business making its first sale.
rationales.security: Stillness and conventional stability frighten him; his father's retirement and Rachel's brother's "401ks" feel more threatening than appealing.
rationales.benevolence: By the end he is searching for a form of motion that "includes her," showing real consideration for Rachel in what comes next.
